6: YHWH’s second appearance to Solomon (v1-9) transforms the dedicatory triumph with an explicit conditional warning: wᵉʾim šôḇ tᵉšûḇûn ʾattem ûḇᵉnêḵem mēʾaḥărî wᵉlōʾ tišmᵉrû miṣwōtay — ‘but if you turn aside, you or your children, from following me and do not keep my commandments.’ The consequence: wᵉhakkrattî ʾet yiśrāʾēl mēʿal pᵉnê hāʾădāmāh — ‘I will cut off Israel from the land.’ The temple itself becomes a negative witness: wᵉhabbayit hazzeh yihyeh ʿelyôn — ‘this house will become a heap of ruins.’ The conditional structure is pure Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 28:15-68; 1 Kings 29:22-28: land forfeited by covenant unfaithfulness), and this passage is the theological key to the entire Kings narrative: every king who violates the covenant moves the temple closer to the fulfillment of v9. The temple is not an unconditional guarantee of YHWH’s protection — it is a conditional gift that YHWH can and will reclaim. Jeremiah 7:1-15 (the ‘Temple Sermon’) applies this text directly in the generation before the temple’s destruction: Jeremiah confronts those trusting in ‘the temple of YHWH, the temple of YHWH, the temple of YHWH’ (Jeremiah 7:4) as a security independent of covenant fidelity. The canonical arc from 1 Kings 9:6-9 to Jer 7 to the exile of 586 BCE demonstrates what the narrator of Kings insists throughout: YHWH fulfilled exactly what he had warned he would do.
1: After completing both the temple and the royal palace and all he desired to build, YHWH appeared to Solomon a second time. The second theophany follows the completion of all major construction projects — a period spanning approximately 20 years (7 years for the temple, 1 Kings 6:38; 13 years for the palace complex, 1 Kings 7:1). The timing is significant: YHWH's response to the prayer of dedication comes after Solomon has finished the entire building program, not immediately following the ceremony.
2: 'YHWH appeared to Solomon a second time, just as he had appeared to him at Gibeon.' The comparison to Gibeon (the first theophany, 1 Kings 3) frames the second appearance as a formal divine response. At Gibeon, YHWH gave Solomon a gift (wisdom) in response to his request; now YHWH gives a conditional covenant in response to the temple's completion and the dedicatory prayer.
3: 'I have heard your prayer and your plea. I have consecrated this house that you have built by putting my name there forever (wəšamtî šəmî šām ləʿôlām).' YHWH's response directly addresses the šēm theology of Solomon's prayer (1 Kings 8:29): the name is placed there as promised. The consecration formula (hiqaddaštî, I have made holy) establishes the temple's sacred status through divine declaration, not merely human construction. 'My eyes and my heart will be there perpetually' — YHWH's attention and relational investment are committed to this location.
4: 'As for you, if you walk before me as your father David walked — with integrity and uprightness (bəṯom-lēḇāḇ ûḇəyōšer), doing all I have commanded you, keeping my statutes and ordinances.' The conditional structure of the Solomonic covenant is now made explicit. The standard of comparison is David's — not David's sinlessness but David's integrity of orientation and responsiveness to divine correction. The contrast with Solomon's future failure (1 Kings 11:4: 'his heart was not wholly devoted to YHWH') is already implicit.
5: 'Then I will establish your royal throne over Israel forever (ləʿôlām), as I promised your father David: You will never lack a man on the throne of Israel.' The unconditional promise (dynasty established forever) and the conditional requirement (walk as David walked) are held in tension. The resolution comes in the Messianic fulfillment — the one descendant of David who perfectly fulfills the condition (Matthew 3:17) and embodies the eternal throne.
7: 'Then I will cut off Israel from the land I have given them, and I will cast from my sight this house I consecrated for my name.' The exile threat uses the Deuteronomic vocabulary: 'cut off' (hikrattî) and 'cast from my sight' (šillahttî). The same house just consecrated (v3) will be rejected; the same name just placed (v3) will be removed. Ezekiel's vision of YHWH's glory departing from the temple (Ezek 10-11) is the fulfillment of this conditional threat — departure of the divine presence before the Babylonian destruction in 586 BCE.
8: 'This exalted house will become a ruin. Everyone who passes by will be appalled (šāmam) and will hiss (šāraq), saying, Why has YHWH done this to this land and to this house?' The prophetic description of the ruined temple anticipates the actual language used in Lamentations and in Jeremiah's temple sermon (Jeremiah 7:12-14). The passer-by astonishment formula appears in 1 Kings 9:8, Jeremiah 18:16; 1 Kings 19:8; 1 Kings 49:17; 1 Kings 50:13; Lamentations 2:15-16 — a recurring literary form for the incomprehensible reversal of divine favor.
9: 'The answer will be: Because they abandoned YHWH their God who brought their fathers out of Egypt, and took hold of other gods and worshipped them and served them.' The question-and-answer structure is a catechetical device for transmitting the theological interpretation of historical catastrophe. The answer is the Deuteronomic formula for the exile: abandonment of YHWH + embrace of other gods = divine judgment. This formula is repeated almost verbatim in 2 Kings 17:7-23 (the interpretation of the northern exile).
10: After twenty years of building — the temple and the royal palace — Solomon gave Hiram twenty cities in the land of Galilee. The twenty-year timeline (7 years for the temple, 1 Kings 6:38; 13 for the palace, 1 Kings 7:1) is confirmed by this administrative note. The Galilee cities represent Solomon's payment for the enormous supply of cedar, cypress, and gold that Hiram had provided through the entire building period.
11: Since Hiram king of Tyre had supplied Solomon with whatever cedar, cypress, and gold he needed, King Solomon gave Hiram twenty cities in the land of Galilee. The commercial arrangement that underpinned the temple construction (1 Kings 5) is now being settled. Tyre's proximity to the Galilee region made these border cities potentially valuable to Hiram as territorial expansion into formerly Israelite land.
12: When Hiram came from Tyre to inspect the towns Solomon had given him, they did not please him. The word lōʾ yāšrû bəʿênāyw (they were not right in his eyes) uses the same evaluative formula used for royal assessments throughout Kings ('right/evil in the eyes of YHWH'). Hiram's disappointment may reflect the towns' poor economic value or agricultural productivity compared to his expectation.
13: Hiram called them 'Cabul' (kāḇûl) — a pun possibly meaning 'like nothing' or 'valueless.' 2 Chronicles 8:2 provides a supplementary note that Solomon eventually recovered these cities; the Cabul episode may reflect an initial payment later renegotiated after Hiram expressed dissatisfaction. Archaeological survey of the Galilee region has identified a site called Kabul that may preserve the ancient name.
14: Hiram had sent the king 120 talents of gold. This is likely the total gold payment accompanying all of Hiram's cedar and material supply — the financial dimension of the commercial arrangement that ran through the entire building program. Combined with the 420 talents from Ophir (v28), Solomon's gold income during this period was enormous.
15: The account of Solomon's forced labor (mas) records the administrative realities behind the building program: the Millo (the terracing/fill structure of Jerusalem's City of David, the Stepped Stone Structure attested in archaeology), the city wall, Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer. These three principal fortifications controlled Israel's major north-south travel corridors. Yadin's 1958-1969 excavations at Hazor and Megiddo identified a distinctive 'Solomonic gate' design at both sites (six-chambered gates), confirmed also at Gezer — striking archaeological confirmation of the Kings narrative.
16: Pharaoh king of Egypt had captured Gezer, burned it, killed the Canaanites living there, and given it as a dowry gift to his daughter — Solomon's wife. The archaeology of Gezer confirms a destruction layer from approximately the 10th century BCE, followed by construction of the characteristic Solomonic gate style. The Pharaoh who gave Gezer as a dowry is unknown but may have been Siamun of the 21st dynasty (ca. 978-959 BCE).
17: Solomon rebuilt Gezer and Lower Beth-horon. Lower Beth-horon (on the western descent from Jerusalem toward the coastal plain) was a strategically critical site controlling the pass from the Aijalon Valley — the same route Joshua used for the battle of Gibeon (Joshua 10:10-11) and where Judas Maccabeus later defeated the Seleucids. Its fortification protected the approach to Jerusalem from the west.
18: Baalath and Tamar in the wilderness. Baalath was in the territory of Dan (Joshua 19:44), possibly near Gezer. Tamar in the Negev is a desert fortification on the route toward Edom and the Red Sea — possibly identified with 'En Hazeva in the Arabah, excavated in the 1990s. These southern fortifications protected the trade routes to Ezion-geber and the Ophir gold source.
19: Solomon's building program included store cities (ʿārê hammiskenôt), chariot cities (ʿārê hāreḵeḇ), and cavalry cities (ʿārê hāpārāšîm) — a three-tier military infrastructure distributed across the kingdom. The Megiddo excavations (Oriental Institute, Chicago) revealed large stable complexes from this period, and the six-chambered gate design appears at multiple sites.
20: The non-Israelite peoples remaining in the land — Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites — were the remnant of the pre-Israelite population that Joshua had not fully destroyed (Josh 9; 1 Kings 11:19-20; Judg 1). Their continued presence centuries after the conquest reflects the incomplete nature of the settlement. Solomon now resolves this through administrative absorption: they become the permanent labor force.
21: 'Their descendants still in the land — those the Israelites had been unable to destroy completely (lōʾ yāḵəlû bənê-yiśrāʾēl ləhaḥărîmām) — Solomon conscripted as forced laborers.' The ḥērem (complete destruction) that Joshua was supposed to apply was not fully executed; the Canaanite remnant became the foundation of Solomon's labor system. The same failure that produced ongoing religious temptation (Judges 2:3) now produces economic utility — a rationalization that the incomplete obedience was providential.
22: 'But Solomon did not make any Israelites into slaves; they served as soldiers, his officials, commanders, captains, and commanders of his chariots and cavalry.' The distinction between Canaanite slaves and Israelite soldiers/officials reflects the official social hierarchy. However, the forced labor system applied to Israelites (1 Kings 5:13-14) was already creating social resentment that would fracture the kingdom in 1 Kings 12 — the Jeroboam-labor narrative (1 Kings 11:28) and the people's complaint to Rehoboam (1 Kings 12:4) reveal the practical gap between the official distinction and daily reality.
23: Five hundred and fifty chief officers supervised Solomon's workforce. The parallel in 5:16 gives 3,300 supervisors, and 2 Chronicles 8:10 gives 250 chief officers — different levels of the supervisory hierarchy are being counted in each passage. The sheer number of supervisors required for Solomon's construction projects indicates the enormous scale of the labor mobilization.
24: Pharaoh's daughter moved from the City of David to her own palace that Solomon had built for her. 2 Chronicles 8:11 explains: 'No wife of mine shall dwell in the house of David king of Israel, for the places to which the ark of YHWH has come are holy.' The Millo construction following her move may indicate the expansion of the city's northern defensive terracing to include the new residential area.
25: Three times a year Solomon offered burnt offerings and peace offerings on the altar — at the three annual pilgrimage feasts (Passover, Weeks, Tabernacles — Deuteronomy 16:16; Exodus 23:14-17). Solomon's observance is presented positively here, before 1 Kings 11 reveals his apostasy. The formal covenant observance continued even as the internal spiritual condition deteriorated.
26: King Solomon built a fleet of ships at Ezion-geber, near Eloth on the shore of the Red Sea, in the land of Edom. Ezion-geber is identified with Tell el-Kheleifeh near modern Aqaba (Jordan)/Eilat (Israel), at the northern end of the Gulf of Aqaba. Nelson Glueck's 1938-1940 excavations identified a substantial site there with evidence of industrial activity. The Red Sea fleet gave Solomon access to the trade routes of the Arabian Peninsula and East Africa, explaining the gold-from-Ophir operations.
27: Hiram sent his own servants — experienced sailors who knew the sea (yōḏəʿê hayyām) — to serve alongside Solomon's men. The Phoenicians (Tyrians) were the Mediterranean world's most experienced sailors; the Israelites had no maritime tradition. The joint venture gives Solomon access to Hiram's seafaring expertise while Hiram gains access to the Red Sea trade routes his Mediterranean fleet could not reach — the same partnership that built the temple now extends to oceanic commerce.
28: They sailed to Ophir and brought back 420 talents of gold to King Solomon. Ophir's location is debated: proposals include southern Arabia (Yemen), East Africa (Somalia/Eritrea/Ethiopia), or India (Gujarat). The 420 talents of gold (about 15 tons) represents an extraordinary commercial return for a single voyage, confirming the Red Sea trade route as one of the ancient world's most lucrative arteries for precious metals.
